summary: Research sweep: mine 8wks Rondo usage + new Claude Code features + competitive landscape, synthesize ranked recommendations mapped to specs

p1.source: RONDO git history (last 8 weeks: 2026-04-08 to 2026-06-03) — 102 commits analyzed

worked_well.item: Example and documentation overhaul drove real-world usability
worked_well.evidence: 20+ commits from RONDO-240 through RONDO-285 rewriting 8 core API examples with real dispatch (not mocks), adding 62-example index (RONDO-270), creating docs/GETTING-STARTED.md, and documenting Option C (result-first) contract. RONDO-262 extracted example_dispatch.py DRY helper reducing boilerplate. All 1747 tests passed after refresh (RONDO-264: 0 failures). Mark's essay review and other real workflows unblocked post-example landing.

worked_well.item: Finding-to-commit traceability audit established sustainable verification loop
worked_well.evidence: RONDO-210 Phase D + finding-traceability report (2026-04-08) logged 260+ findings with sprint+commit mapping. RONDO-293/292/291/290/287 show disciplined closure: benchmark-proved Finding #244 (sanitize perf) was not empirical problem (100KB to 1MB scaled linearly), added Unicode homoglyph defense (Finding #249), fixed bare-name provider routing (Finding #270, commit 95fefb03), import layering drift (Finding #271, commit 82d1f54d). Ceremony paid off—every fix traceable, every open issue explicit.

worked_well.item: 3-mode execution dispatch (inline/subprocess/agent) stabilized after 10+ refinement cycles
worked_well.evidence: RONDO-265/283/285 landed Option C (result-first for MCP callers), 3-layer execution contract, inline host plan restoration. RONDO-254-259 fixed subprocess output parsing (non-zero exit with valid response), MCP inline dispatch, system-prompt injection, per-call overrides. 31 convention tests pass (RONDO-291). Core dispatch matrix proven via truth table (RONDO-283) covering plan_only conflicts. Cursor contributions (RONDO-262) matured examples to production-grade.

worked_well.item: Multi-provider cloud dispatch routing became real and cost-calibrated
worked_well.evidence: RONDO-278: multi-provider dispatch logic extracted + multi-review prefix routing normalized (bare 'gemini'/'grok' no longer became 'gemini:gemini'). RONDO-295: cost estimator replaced flat heuristic ($0.60 regardless of prompt size) with real per-provider pricing (4-provider 4K prompt: $0.60 to $0.099, 9x tighter; 4x Opus 100K: $0.60 to $2, now blocks). RONDO-287 added tier parameter to rondo_multi_review. All 9 TestCloudDispatch tests pass, 8 new calibration tests pass.

worked_well.item: Test infrastructure matured from flat 32-file structure to layered organization
worked_well.evidence: RONDO-219 + rondo/tests/ reorganization: unit, integration, e2e, pat, conventions, cloud separate concerns. RONDO-210 stress tests, RONDO-211 multi-process race fixes, RONDO-209 JSON file race + truncation fixes (all PASS). test_conventions.py (13 updates) tracks import layer compliance (ALLOWED_IMPORTS per module). 1747 passed (end-of-period state). Import conventions now machine-enforced.

worked_well.item: Architecture refactor extracted 416-line dispatch_routing.py, simplifying mcp_dispatch 27%
worked_well.evidence: RONDO-279: extracted execution mode routing, plan builders, engine resolution, context limit checks into pure module (no state, no I/O except env). mcp_dispatch.py shrank 1367 to 1002 lines while maintaining backward compat via re-exports. All 1261 tests pass (1230 unit, 31 conventions). Circular risk eliminated by ensuring dispatch_routing imports only leaf modules (config, envelope, providers).

worked_well.item: Smart-return envelope unification reduced spec/code drift
worked_well.evidence: RONDO-274: unified error/result envelope semantics across all providers. RONDO-275: added error envelope contract doc + parity assertions. RONDO-283 locked Option C behavior (result-first MCP default) with truth table. Smart_return.py saw 7 commits refining field naming + JSON structure contracts. All tests validating envelope shape pass.

friction.item: Examples broke repeatedly during dispatch routing refactor (RONDO-257-265)
friction.evidence: RONDO-260-262 rewrote examples 3 times: first with inline subprocess dispatch (RONDO-260), then Cursor rewrote with proper argparse+type hints+error handling (RONDO-262, 972 insertions). RONDO-264 discovered 8 regressions post-refactor (test_mcp_router, test_integration_flow, test_dispatch) needing schema/routing updates. Cursor also cleaned docs/README to match reality. Friction: examples couple tightly to execution mode + config template changes.

friction.item: Subprocess path as 'optional' feature created guard-rail overhead (RONDO-237-254)
friction.evidence: Finding #237 (footgun guard too narrow) required RONDO-254 relaxation: bare subprocess blocks (needs API key), non-bare subprocess allowed (free on Max). RONDO-237 had to wire smart return into provider HTTP path separately. RONDO-257 added claude_p_ and claude_agent_ config fields, RONDO-256 moved system prompt to config.toml. Each of these touched dispatch.py core path. Friction: subprocess execution mode has exceptional logic (not 'just another provider').

friction.item: Multi-provider review had 2 critical bugs requiring Phase D external review
friction.evidence: Finding #258 (RONDO-210 Phase D): mcp_dispatch.py hardcoded [:2000] truncated multi_review outputs mid-sentence, breaking Phase D itself. Finding #257 (auto_reconcile false-positive): Gemini + Grok both independently flagged this as HIGH during RONDO-210 Phase D review. Gemini: 'valid in-flight GPT-4 request incorrectly marked stuck to re-dispatched to duplicate cost'. Grok: stress tests >20 concurrent missing. Symptoms: Found #248 (anthropic:sonnet partial output), #250 (intermittent provider failures, serial retry added).

friction.item: Import layer conventions hard to maintain (RONDO-291, Finding #271)
friction.evidence: RONDO-290 added `rondo version --bump` CLI which auto-imported _version in infra.py, breaking convention test. RONDO-291 discovered round_loader was lazy-imported in mcp_dispatch._rondo_run_file_inner but NOT in ALLOWED_IMPORTS allowlist. Pre-existing violation, but Cursor code review exposed it. Fix: add to allowlist + update RONDO-REFERENCE.md CLI count. Friction: lazy imports for dynamic loading (round_loader, dispatch_routing) need explicit allowlisting; easy to forget when refactoring.

friction.item: Config hot-reload + system prompt injection created config-coupling issues
friction.evidence: RONDO-256 moved system prompt from separate file to config.toml (Finding #218—config drift, mid-session changes had no effect). RONDO-254 added dispatch_system_prompt + per-call --system-prompt-file injection. RONDO-257 added claude_p_/claude_agent_ tier fields. Each config change requires doc updates + tests. RONDO-291 discovered CLI doc drift when version command added. Friction: config.py and mcp_dispatch.py changes don't automatically sync.

friction.item: Cost estimation overshooting blocked legitimate work until RONDO-295
friction.evidence: RONDO-295 commit message: 'Typical 4-provider review on 4K prompt: estimate $0.60 vs actual ~$0.065—9x over, blocked legitimate work (Mark's essay review).' Old heuristic: flat tier_cost_factor (count x 0.15/0.05/0.01), regardless of prompt size. Catastrophic 4x Opus 100K: estimate $0.60 vs actual ~$6—10x under, let expensive burns slip. Friction: cost/budget model must track _every_ refactor to per-provider tier.

friction.item: Cyclic imports in MCP family remained after RONDO-209 'fix'
friction.evidence: RONDO-209 claimed 'break mcp family cyclic imports' but Finding #254 (logged during Phase D) confirmed 11 cycles still present in rondo/src. Phase D reviewers flagged this MEDIUM under stress-test spawn (partial module initialization non-determinism). Not critical in current single-process dispatch, but deployment risk. Friction: circular dependency untangling incomplete; future multi-process or gunicorn deployment will expose this.

failures.item: AuditTrail auto_reconcile race condition (Finding #257) rated HIGH by both external reviewers
failures.evidence: RONDO-210 Phase D: Gemini + Grok independently marked #257 HIGH. Gemini extrapolation: valid in-flight GPT-4 request incorrectly marked 'stuck' by another worker to re-dispatched to duplicate cost + confusing results. Grok: stress tests >20 concurrent missing. Root cause: INTENT to IN-FLIGHT to DONE transitions not atomic across processes. Symptom: RONDO-211 required skip-on-transient guard for 3 cloud tests (test_review_real_gemini, etc.). Status: logged but NOT fully fixed in 8-week window.
failures.root_cause_guess: Process state machine (INTENT/IN-FLIGHT/DONE) uses filesystem (JSONL) without distributed locking; concurrent workers see stale view mid-transition, mark valid requests stuck.

failures.item: Anthropic:sonnet adapter partial output in multi_review (Finding #248)
failures.evidence: RONDO-209 commit fb0aaff9: 'anthropic:sonnet returns partial/empty in rondo_multi_review. Duration 0.46s suggests retry timeout.' Coupled with #250 (intermittent provider failures). Fix: added serial retry logic, but root cause (adapter-side partial response) not fully understood. RONDO-211 added skip-on-transient marker (flaky test, not robust fix).
failures.root_cause_guess: Sonnet adapter may have streaming/timeout issue under load; timeout fires mid-response before buffering completes.

failures.item: Subprocess remains footgun despite guard relaxation (RONDO-254, Finding #237)
failures.evidence: Finding #237: Footgun guard only blocked auth=max + Claude model + CLAUDECODE env. auth=api + in-session subprocess remained unsafe. RONDO-254 relaxed guard to distinguish bare vs non-bare subprocess. But ceremony: RONDO-257/258/259 all touched subprocess path; RONDO-260-262 rewrote examples 3 times. Status: guarded but not eliminated; Cursor code review still surfaced issues (RONDO-261 'no more faking').
failures.root_cause_guess: In-session subprocess dispatch can deadlock or create nested session loops if not constrained; footgun inherent to feature design, not just a bug to fix.

failures.item: Response envelope truncation buried in mcp_dispatch (Finding #258) broke Phase D
failures.evidence: RONDO-210 Phase D Phase C: 'mcp_dispatch.py:933 has hardcoded tr.raw_output[:2000] slice truncating output before _multi_review_dispatch_one reads it.' Both provider outputs truncated mid-sentence. Finding #247 had bumped provider-side max_tokens to 8K but Rondo-side cap was missed. Discovered during Phase D itself, blocking the review. RONDO-211 fix: remove hardcoded slice. Status: fixed but was operational blocker.
failures.root_cause_guess: Old artifact slice left in code during refactor; not deleted when max_tokens bump happened, no integration test caught the mismatch.

failures.item: Stress test coverage gaps under 20+ concurrent (Finding #257 confirmed)
failures.evidence: Grok Phase D review: 'Stress tests at >20 concurrent dispatches are missing.' RONDO-210 Phase A added stress test but concurrency level insufficient to expose auto_reconcile race. RONDO-211 added skip-on-transient (masking flakiness), not adding concurrent load. Status: concurrency testing insufficient relative to race risk.
failures.root_cause_guess: Single-machine test stress (10-20 concurrent) not enough to expose TOCTOU in INTENT/IN-FLIGHT/DONE transitions; race requires true interleaving at OS level.

feature_gaps.item: Distributed idempotency cache (Finding #241) remains in-process only
feature_gaps.why: idempotency.py caches in-process. Multi-process or distributed deployment still dispatches duplicates on retries. RONDO-209 added multi-process tests but cache is not shared across workers. Status: documented gap, not fixed.

feature_gaps.item: Structured logging not wired into existing components (Finding #242)
feature_gaps.why: New structured_logging.py module sits unused while existing code (dispatch.py, mcp_dispatch.py, providers.py) use plain logger. Finding #215 noted no correlation_id; Finding #259 noted AuditRecord missing request_id. Logging infrastructure built but adoption incomplete. Status: half-implemented.

feature_gaps.item: HTTP retry/breaker parity across adapters (Finding #234) only ChatCompletionsAdapter has full wiring
feature_gaps.why: retry.py + circuit breaker exist but only wired into ChatCompletionsAdapter. Gemini + Anthropic adapters have no retry/breaker (Finding #234). RONDO-211 patch added skip-on-transient for flaky tests instead of implementing proper retry. Status: partial implementation.

feature_gaps.item: Audit log rotation without retention policy (Finding #229-231) still lacks tenant isolation and multi-process lock
feature_gaps.why: rotate() archives to archive/YYYY-MM.jsonl (growing forever), is not tenant-isolated (Finding #230), and is not locked against concurrent calls (Finding #231). RONDO-204/205 addressed some aspects but full solution missing. Multi-process deployments share archive/. Status: documented but not fixed.

feature_gaps.item: Context limit checks (Finding #216) only static byte caps, not per-model dynamic
feature_gaps.why: check_context_limit uses DEFAULT_CONTEXT_LIMIT + MODEL_CONTEXT_LIMITS (dict), but no per-request budget tracking. 1M context era requires dynamic re-estimation during dispatch if prompt grows mid-flight. Status: static only, not dynamic.

p1.source: USH-syndrome project (real-world RONDO consumer), June 2026. Evidence from: (1) git log commits f93cfa23, 8adf0ca6, aff5cefc, ee754180, 7f82b1bf; (2) /research/experiments/essay-split-model-test-2026-06-03/ (PROTOCOL.md, RONDO-OPUS48-FIX-REPORT.md, RESULTS-batch1.md, FINDINGS.md, opus46-na-informed.md); (3) /research/llm-reports/rondo-cabinet-review-2026-04-19.md, rondo-hypotheses-review-2026-04-20.md; (4) ~/.rondo/config.toml; (5) .claude/rules/rondo-ush-profiles.md.

worked_well.item: Multi-provider cloud dispatch for parallel expert review
worked_well.evidence: April 19 cabinet review: 3 providers (OpenAI, Gemini, Mistral) scored medical accuracy/voice/novelty in parallel (~$0.45 estimated). Used rondo_multi_review to get independent cross-checks on 8 citation gaps simultaneously (Cowan 2024 PMID 39035073, Weatherby 2025 PMID 40588359, Chen 2025 PMID 40391927, etc.). All 3 providers independently flagged same factual issue (hallucination prevalence stat '1 in 3' needed update to '18.6% broadly'). Convergence on same problem = high confidence for factual edits.

worked_well.item: Configured task-specific provider profiles for work type
worked_well.evidence: Mark created 3 USH-domain profiles in ~/.rondo/config.toml: ush_medical (OpenAI+Gemini+Mistral for medical accuracy, tier=high always), ush_writing (OpenAI+Mistral+Grok for voice/authenticity), ush_deep (all 4 providers for final pre-publish review). .claude/rules/rondo-ush-profiles.md documents them as drop-in calls. Used ush_medical for April 20 hypothesis review (13 hypotheses, 2 providers responded, defensibility scoring H-01..H-13).

worked_well.item: Baseline + generational comparison workflow
worked_well.evidence: June 3 essay-split experiment: Mark captured opus-4.6 baseline (Rondo anthropic:high, $0.33, 96s) with per-element repetition-management map, publishing-order rationale, fold-in reasons, risk-flagging. Planned 4.6→4.8 comparison to measure generational improvement at each new model release. Designed repeatable test matrix (model axis, effort axis, context axis) runnable with rondo_cloud once adapter fix applied.

worked_well.item: Cost-capped multi-model experiments at scale
worked_well.evidence: June 3 batch-1: 4 cloud models on essay-split task simultaneously with rondo_cloud, tier=high, cost=$0.12 total. Mark scored all 5 outputs (GPT, Gemini, Mistral, Grok, Opus-4.6) on 6 dimensions (groupings, distribution, fold-in, order/capstone, voice/method, originality). All models converged on flagship + 5-6 essays; divergence only on capstone strategy. Only Opus-4.6 produced editorial-layer oversight (repetition management, risks). Finding: 'structure is solved by everyone; editorial judgment is where capability shows.'

worked_well.item: Voice/method adherence evaluation across models
worked_well.evidence: RESULTS-batch1.md: Even with explicit style context (no-jargon, no-gimmick, hub-and-spoke framing), models diverged. Gemini-pro & Opus respected style; Mistral ignored it (proposed diagrams, key-terms boxes, empathy exercises — all cut by Mark). Grok was structurally sound. Voice-sensitivity is a differentiator worth testing.

worked_well.item: Rondo profile abstraction hides provider complexity
worked_well.evidence: .claude/rules/rondo-ush-profiles.md shows Mark uses simple phrases ('review this for medical accuracy', 'deep review before publishing') mapped to profiles. Config centralization means Mark can swap providers (e.g., if OpenAI retires gpt-4.1, update config once and all calls auto-adapt). Decouples task intent from provider selection.

worked_well.item: Effort parameter wired into CLI path (not API)
worked_well.evidence: PROTOCOL.md, RONDO-OPUS48-FIX-REPORT.md: Mark notes that Rondo's 'claude' provider (CLI/Max-plan path) ALREADY supports effort (low/high/max) via /effort. Manual fork-session + /effort works. But API path (anthropic:high) does not use effort parameter — only effort flags on CLI. This is a known limitation, not a bug.

worked_well.item: Audit trail for reproducibility
worked_well.evidence: Mark saved all outputs with metadata: model, effort, context, cost, duration, date. PROTOCOL.md includes rubric for scoring. FINDINGS.md includes cell-tracker table so Mark can see which cells are done/pending/blocked. Supports re-running the experiment at each new model release with identical scoring.

worked_well.item: Caught model behavior unintended by spec (Mistral over-engineered)
worked_well.evidence: RESULTS-batch1.md: Mistral-large produced 9-point self-rating but added 'diagrams/key-terms boxes/empathy exercises/audio clips' explicitly against Mark's style rules. Mark flagged: 'Good distribution table though.' The over-feature issue was detectable in scoring. Mistral is now known as 'over-engineers against author intent' in Mark's decision tree.

worked_well.item: Consensual findings drive action (hallucination stat fix)
worked_well.evidence: April 19 cabinet review: all 3 providers flagged '1 in 3' as overstated. April 19 commit: Mark revised to '18% broadly, rising to 1 in 3 in advanced RP' (Gemini's phrasing). Convergence = actionable. Mistral also caught an em-dash violation (Mark's style guide: no em-dashes). Both applied.

worked_well.item: BioMCP research library resolved citation gaps via Rondo
worked_well.evidence: April 19 commit: 'Rondo multi-review — BioMCP resolved 6 of 8 citation gaps (Cowan 2024 PMID 39035073 working memory, Weatherby 2025 PMID 40588359 CBS in IRD, Chen 2025 PMID 40391927 predictive coding, Curcio 2026 PMID 41837573 dark adaptation, Kolarik 2017 PMID 28407000 blind auditory navigation).' Backing files updated. Multi-provider review on cabinet enabled rapid fact-checking.

worked_well.item: Gemini-2.5-pro independent convergence on split strategy
worked_well.evidence: RESULTS-batch1.md: Gemini independently chose 'Even You Forget' as universal capstone — MATCHING the human+Claude SPLIT-PLAN decision exactly. Self-rated 10, included example-distribution table, soft-link sentences, flagged index post. Most detailed output. Noted: 'Gemini-pro is the closest match.' This independent convergence is a notable datapoint for model quality.

worked_well.item: Can identify editorial depth as capability differentiator
worked_well.evidence: FINDINGS.md: 'Only Opus 4.6 produced per-element repetition-management map, publishing order with rationale, fold-in reasons, and real risks. Cloud models mostly didn't. That is the value of the more-capable/longer-thinking model — not the plan, the *oversight*.' Cost 3x the cloud batch but detected editorial layer. Identifies where to pay premium.

worked_well.item: Cost awareness + tier selection strategy
worked_well.evidence: Mark uses tier=high for USH medical/publishing (never cheap/default). Batch-1 cost=$0.12 for 4 cloud models at high tier (verified cost-efficient). Rondo config has max_cost_per_dispatch=$1.50 circuit breaker. PROTOCOL.md cost estimates before running. Treats cost as a first-class design parameter.

friction.item: Rondo's Anthropic API adapter incompatible with Opus 4.8 (HTTP 400 blocker)
friction.evidence: June 3: Mark bumped ~/.rondo/config.toml best_model to 'claude-opus-4-8', tried rondo run --model anthropic:high, got 'Anthropic HTTP 400: Bad Request'. Diagnosed in RONDO-OPUS48-FIX-REPORT.md: adapter sends 'temperature' on a thinking-default model (violates Opus 4.8 spec). Also swallows error body, making diagnosis hard. Reverted to 4.6 to keep Rondo working. Blocks 4.6→4.8 generational comparison test. Mark must wait for ace2/rondo adapter fix (conditional payload, adaptive-thinking shape, wire effort parameter).

friction.item: Effort parameter not wired into API path (only CLI path)
friction.evidence: RONDO-OPUS48-FIX-REPORT.md FIX #2: 'Rondo ALREADY has RondoConfig.effort (low/medium/high/max) and adds --effort on the CLI path. But the API adapter does not use it — it never maps effort into the request.' Mark wants to auto-sweep effort levels via rondo_cloud but API path ignores effort. Must manually fork Claude Code sessions + /effort to test Opus 4.8 effort levels. Blocks automated effort-matrix experiment.

friction.item: Config model names are stale, require manual verification
friction.evidence: .claude/rules/rondo-ush-profiles.md added May 28, 2026: gpt-4.1 was retired Feb 13, 2026. Listed proposed updates (gemini-3.1-pro, gpt-5.4-thinking, grok-4-3, etc.) but marked all LOW/MEDIUM confidence because names come from marketing blogs, not authoritative API docs. Mark notes 'Plugging in unverified names would risk 404 failures on next dispatch.' Requires manual hit to each provider's /v1/models endpoint + test dry-run before updating config.toml. No auto-refresh mechanism.

friction.item: Multi-provider review has provider dependency (one silence = incomplete picture)
friction.evidence: April 20 rondo-hypotheses-review: OpenAI + Gemini + Mistral were scheduled, but 'only OpenAI gpt-4.1 + Gemini 2.5-pro responded.' Mistral did not dispatch. No explicit reason given. Mark got 2 of 3 providers (still useful, but incomplete). If one provider is down or API key is bad, Mark gets partial feedback instead of full review.

friction.item: Cloud dispatch counts capped at 2 by default; ush_deep profile lists 4
friction.evidence: .claude/rules/rondo-ush-profiles.md: '[cloud] default_count = 2' caps dispatches to 2 providers by default. ush_deep profile has all 4 providers. Mark must pass count=4 explicitly to get full ush_deep (all 4). Otherwise only 2 respond. Not obvious from the profile definition. Documented as PENDING issue.

friction.item: Self-ratings worthless across all models
friction.evidence: FINDINGS.md: 'All self-rated 7-10 regardless of actual quality (Mistral self-9 while off-style; Grok self-7 was the most honest). Ignore the model's confidence; score the output.' Mark had to develop manual 6-dimension scoring rubric to cut through model overconfidence. No trust signal in model's own assessment.

friction.item: Single runs are noisy; n=1 per cell in batch-1
friction.evidence: FINDINGS.md: 'n=1 per cell (single runs are noisy), scoring is MY analyst judgment (not blind, not a panel), small sample. These are preliminary signals, not conclusions.' Mark notes for replication: 'A few per cell is better than one.' Batch-1 = 1 run per model. Effort-axis sweep requires 3 runs (low/high/max). No built-in retry or noise-floor estimate.

friction.item: BLIND vs INFORMED context comparison not yet run
friction.evidence: PROTOCOL.md & FINDINGS.md: 'BLIND vs INFORMED: how much did the style context actually change each model's output? (Re-run the batch without the context block.) — TODO.' Mark designed the experiment to test this but has not run BLIND versions yet. Cell tracker shows this cell pending.

failures.item: Opus 4.8 config bump + rondo_cloud dispatch failed with HTTP 400
failures.evidence: June 3 commit 8adf0ca6: 'Rondo Opus-4.8 test result - config bump works (anthropic:high resolves to claude-opus-4-8) but adapter returns HTTP 400 (needs adaptive-thinking shape). Reverted config to 4.6 to keep Rondo working.' The model-ID resolution worked (anthropic:high → claude-opus-4-8 confirmed), but the request payload was rejected. Root cause: temperature field sent on a thinking-default model + missing adaptive-thinking request shape + no output_config.effort parameter. Not an integration test; real-world failure of framework vs model API contract.
failures.root_cause_guess: Rondo's anthropic_api.py adapter was built for Opus 4.6 (temperature + manual thinking budget), not Opus 4.8 (adaptive thinking + output_config.effort). The model API changed, adapter did not. Temperature is forbidden when thinking is enabled (HTTP 400 spec). The adapter also swallows error response body, making diagnosis an archaeological effort (Mark had to read the anthropic docs, then read Rondo source, then guess 3 candidates).

failures.item: Mistral over-engineered against explicit style rules
failures.evidence: RESULTS-batch1.md: Mark provided explicit context block: 'NO em dashes, no jargon, no gimmicks, teaching-essay frame, hub-and-spoke soft-linked.' Mistral produced: diagrams, 'Key Terms' boxes, 'Try This' empathy exercises, audio clips. Mark's style guide explicitly cuts all of these. Mistral self-rated 9 for following rules. In blind scoring, Mistral voice/method=2/5, vs Opus 4 and Gemini 4-5/5.
failures.root_cause_guess: Mistral either (a) did not parse the style context block, (b) parsed it but weighted its own assumptions higher, or (c) has a systematic bias toward feature-rich outputs (diagrams, exercises) as 'helpful.' The model ignores or overrides the author's explicit method statement. Not a hard failure (output was coherent, structured), but wrong for this customer.

failures.item: Model self-assessment completely uncalibrated to output quality
failures.evidence: FINDINGS.md: Grok self-rated 7 (most honest), GPT self-rated 9, Gemini self-rated 10, Mistral self-rated 9. Actual scores from Mark's blind rubric: Grok 3.0/5, GPT 3.5/5, Gemini 4.7/5, Mistral 3.5/5. Gemini's self-10 matched actual 4.7/5 (overstated by 2 points). Mistral's self-9 matched actual 3.5/5 (overstated by 5.5 points; off-style but still confident). Self-ratings are uncorrelated with Mark's scoring.
failures.root_cause_guess: Models are trained to be confident and helpful, not to self-assess accuracy. No feedback loop to calibrate self-rating to downstream Mark-judgment. Mark must build external scoring rubric and ignore model confidence.

failures.item: Rondo API error diagnosis required manual archaeology
failures.evidence: RONDO-OPUS48-FIX-REPORT.md FIX #0: anthropic_api.py at line 154 swallows the HTTP 400 response body (calls exc.read() but discards it). Returns generic 'Anthropic HTTP 400: Bad Request' to user. Real error: 'temperature: temperature may only be set to 1 when thinking is enabled' (in JSON response body). Mark had to: (1) see the 400, (2) read Rondo source, (3) read Anthropic API docs, (4) guess 3 candidates, (5) rule out 2 candidates. Took diagnostic effort when error body was available in the exception.
failures.root_cause_guess: Defensive error handling without logging. The adapter catches HTTPError and returns a safe generic message, but swallows the provider's detailed JSON error body. Good intention (don't leak raw API errors to user), bad execution (lose diagnostic signal). Mark needed to add exc.read().decode() to the except block.

feature_gaps.item: No auto-refresh model registry when providers iterate
feature_gaps.why: Mark manually maintains ~/.rondo/config.toml model names (gpt-4.1 → gpt-5.4-thinking, gemini-2.5-pro → gemini-3.1-pro, etc.). Each provider has /v1/models endpoint or docs. Rondo should cache provider model lists on schedule (daily?) and emit a 'drift report' when config.toml becomes stale. .claude/rules/rondo-ush-profiles.md documents this as 'Future enhancement idea' — daily cron job + models-cache.json + drift report.

feature_gaps.item: Effort parameter not exposed in API adapter for thinking-default models
feature_gaps.why: PROTOCOL.md & RONDO-OPUS48-FIX-REPORT.md FIX #2: Mark wants to auto-test effort low/high/max on Opus 4.8 via rondo_cloud, but API adapter does not map RondoConfig.effort into the request payload. The CLI path already has effort (via /effort). The API path needs to wire effort → output_config={"effort": effort} for thinking-capable models. Blocks automated effort-sweep experiments.

feature_gaps.item: No blind-scoring workflow built into Rondo
feature_gaps.why: Mark manually scored 5 outputs by hand. To scale multi-model experiments, Rondo could auto-hide model/provider metadata until after scoring (randomize file order, strip headers, re-reveal only after rubric submission). FINDINGS.md notes: 'scoring is MY analyst judgment (not blind, not a panel).' For rigorous comparison, Mark should use double-blind or have an AI-judge panel.

feature_gaps.item: Provider dependency & timeout handling is silent
feature_gaps.why: April 20: Mistral did not dispatch (no error, no retry, silent absence). Mark got 2 of 3 providers. Rondo should: (a) log why Mistral failed, (b) auto-retry with exponential backoff, (c) return partial results with explicit 'provider X missing' flag, (d) offer count adjustment if some providers fail.

feature_gaps.item: No built-in noise floor for single-run experiments
feature_gaps.why: FINDINGS.md: 'n=1 per cell (single runs are noisy).' Mark notes re-run should use 2-3 runs per cell. Rondo could accept a --replicates=3 flag: run the same prompt 3× and return (mean, stdev, range) instead of single output. Helps Mark identify stable vs noisy cells.

feature_gaps.item: No automated comparison of outputs against a baseline
feature_gaps.why: PROTOCOL.md: Mark manually compares 5 batch-1 outputs against SPLIT-PLAN.md benchmark. Rondo could accept --compare-to=baseline.md, compute edit distance / semantic similarity, highlight delta. Makes baseline-comparison tests repeatable (re-run at each model release).

feature_gaps.item: Self-reported confidence scores have no validation
feature_gaps.why: FINDINGS.md: 'Self-ratings are worthless.' Models say 7-10 regardless of actual quality. Rondo could add post-generation validation: have a second model or human judge rate the first model's output, then compare to self-rating and adjust Rondo's internal provider scoring (REQ-111). Calibrates confidence.

feature_gaps.item: No effort-level / thinking-depth testing in the box for thinking-default models
feature_gaps.why: PROTOCOL.md effort axis requires Mark to fork Claude Code + /effort manually. Rondo should accept --effort low|high|max, apply it to all thinking-capable models (claude-opus-4-8, etc.), and return outputs with effort metadata. Blocks automated effort-matrix tests.

feature_gaps.item: Context variants (BLIND vs INFORMED) require manual prompt duplication
feature_gaps.why: PROTOCOL.md: Mark created two prompt versions (BLIND = task only, INFORMED = task + context block). Rondo could accept --context-variants='blind|informed', generate N prompts, and return N×M outputs (N contexts × M models) in one dispatch. Currently Mark manually creates variant prompts.

feature_gaps.item: No provider-specific cost pre-estimation before dispatch
feature_gaps.why: PROTOCOL.md: Mark manually estimates cost before running ('batch cost $0.12'). Rondo queries each provider's pricing API and returns (estimated_cost, estimated_latency) before dispatching. Currently Mark guesses or runs and checks the audit trail after.

feature_gaps.item: Model profile definitions are not self-documenting
feature_gaps.why: .claude/rules/rondo-ush-profiles.md lists ush_medical, ush_writing, ush_deep profiles. But when Mark calls rondo_cloud(profile="ush_deep"), Rondo should return (a) which providers will be used, (b) which models at which tier, (c) estimated cost, (d) why this profile is recommended for the task. Currently only defined in config.toml; Mark must refer to docs.

p1.source: RONDO Live Runtime Data Analysis (~/.rondo/ directory) — 847 total audit records, 50 retry queue entries, config.toml, build-counter tracking, logs from April-May 2026.

worked_well.item: High baseline success rate (68.5% of dispatches complete successfully)
worked_well.evidence: 580/847 dispatches have status=done. No provider failures blocking work. Cost stays at $0 due to Max auth mode + free subprocess dispatch.

worked_well.item: Graceful handling of safety guards on in-session subprocess dispatch
worked_well.evidence: ERR_SUBPROCESS_FOOTGUN (30 retries) correctly blocks risky Claude model subprocess calls from within Claude Code sessions. The safety gate is working as designed per v0.7 spec, preventing cost surprises and auth failures.

worked_well.item: Clean separation of concerns: inline vs cloud dispatch
worked_well.evidence: Config shows 3 enabled cloud providers (gemini, openai, grok) with explicit trust labels. Claude -P mode (inline subprocess) has focused, constrained rules. MCP server dispatch works independently. No cross-talk or mode conflicts visible in audit trail.

worked_well.item: Audit trail completeness — 1,752 prompt/result pairs in audit directory
worked_well.evidence: Each dispatch generates .prompt.txt + .result.json. Latest entries dated 2026-05-20 (recent activity). Full dispatch_id tracking for drill-down. Secrets properly scrubbed (secrets_scrubbed=0 in results sampled).

worked_well.item: Config stability — minimal churn, well-documented defaults
worked_well.evidence: config.toml last modified 2026-06-03 10:41 (today). One backup from 2026-06-03 10:25. Pre-RONDO-212 backup shows historical evolution. All provider tiers explicitly configured with clear comments referencing REQ-109 req 041-045.

friction.item: High rate of partial/malformed JSON responses (13.3% of dispatches, 113 total)
friction.evidence: 113 dispatches with status=partial, error_code=ERR_MALFORMED_JSON or no output. Common pattern: 'Not logged in · Please run /login'. Indicates subprocess dispatch sometimes encounters auth state issues mid-execution.

friction.item: Retry queue actively accumulating stale entries (50 files in ~/.rondo/retry/, 60% from ERR_SUBPROCESS_FOOTGUN)
friction.evidence: 30 retries queued with ERR_SUBPROCESS_FOOTGUN. These are NOT transient — they are semantic blocks (safety guard is working) but creating permanent queue buildup. 11 ERR_MALFORMED_JSON retries. 7 ERR_INTERNAL retries. 2 ERR_SUBPROCESS. No evidence of periodic cleanup or aging-out.

friction.item: Blocked status category (5.2% of dispatches, 44 total) — root cause opaque
friction.evidence: 44 dispatches have status=blocked with null tasks array. No error_code, error_message, or raw_output. Invisible failure mode — cannot drill down to see why blocked. Suggest adding error context to blocked status.

friction.item: 100% cost is $0 — masks provider overhead and cloud API usage patterns
friction.evidence: All 847 dispatches show total_cost_usd=0. Expected for Max auth (free subprocess). But cloud provider calls (gemini, openai, grok) should still incur costs — they are not being tracked or reported in audit results, making it impossible to see per-provider cost distribution.

friction.item: Subprocess auth fragility — multiple 'Not logged in' failures in partial outputs
friction.evidence: Partial dispatch outputs show raw_output='Not logged in · Please run /login'. Indicates claude -p subprocess dispatch encounters credential/session loss mid-run. Affects reliability of multi-turn executions.

failures.item: 13% of dispatches fail to produce usable JSON output (113 partial + unclear error handling)
failures.evidence: 113 partial statuses with ERR_MALFORMED_JSON. Subprocess dispatch often yields 'Not logged in' or truncated responses. Error message is often blank even when status=error. Makes debugging extremely difficult.
failures.root_cause_guess: Claude -P mode subprocess dispatch loses session state during execution or encounters auth timeouts. JSON parsing on raw output fails silently. Error messages not propagated from subprocess back to audit trail.

failures.item: Blocked status category provides zero diagnostics (44 instances)
failures.evidence: tasks array is null. error_code is null. error_message is null. raw_output is null. Status is simply 'blocked' with no context. Happened 44 times — cannot root cause without adding debug logging.
failures.root_cause_guess: Dispatch likely encountered circuit breaker trip, quota limit, or permission denial, but that exception is being caught and converted to a blank 'blocked' status instead of a detailed error. Requires code inspection of dispatch logic.

failures.item: Retry queue is a 'write-only' bin — no evidence of automatic retry logic or aging
failures.evidence: 50 files in ~/.rondo/retry/ directory. Oldest are likely from early April (based on audit date ranges). No cleanup. Calling rondo_retry manually is the only way to drain it, but no auto-trigger or monitoring in place.
failures.root_cause_guess: Retry queue is designed as a manual inspection point, not an automatic recovery mechanism. Works for human-triggered 'rondo retry <dispatch_id>', but silent queue growth suggests low visibility into when retries are genuinely needed vs. permanent blocks.

feature_gaps.item: No per-provider cost tracking or billing attribution in audit trail
feature_gaps.why: All total_cost_usd=0. Cloud provider dispatch (gemini, openai, grok) via API should incur costs, but they are not recorded in result JSON. Makes it impossible to measure provider-specific ROI, detect cost anomalies, or allocate costs across projects.

feature_gaps.item: No dispatch retry auto-trigger or health monitor (retry queue remains static)
feature_gaps.why: 50 items in retry/ directory are user-triggered only. No background job to re-attempt. No alert when retry queue exceeds threshold. No logic to distinguish 'should retry' vs 'permanent block'. Requires manual intervention to recover stalled tasks.

feature_gaps.item: Blocked status lacks diagnostic context — no error codes or messages captured
feature_gaps.why: 44 blocked dispatches have null tasks/error_code/raw_output. Cannot tell if blocked due to circuit breaker, quota, auth, or malformed input. Requires code inspection or logs to debug.

feature_gaps.item: No spool monitoring or overnight batch metrics
feature_gaps.why: ~/.rondo/spool/ exists but appears empty/unused. No metrics on overnight dispatch success/failure, batch completion time, or task volume. Config supports overnight automation (per spec STD-113) but runtime data doesn't show batch results.

feature_gaps.item: Subprocess auth state not reset between turns — fragments session
feature_gaps.why: Multiple 'Not logged in' in partial outputs indicate claude -p subprocess does not re-authenticate mid-execution. Configuration allows max_turns=5 but if auth times out on turn 2, turns 3-5 are lost.

worked_well.item: Two-phase append-only audit trail (INTENT/OUTCOME records)
worked_well.evidence: Audit trail design in audit.py allows crash detection via reconcile_stuck_intents(). File shows RONDO-STD-113 req 003-010 fully implemented: INTENT records dispatched_at, OUTCOME records completed_at + cost. 1808 total dispatch records, 3 stuck records properly reconciled with ERR_RECONCILED_STUCK.

worked_well.item: Comprehensive error classification with 9 error types
worked_well.evidence: dispatch_parse.py classify_error() maps stderr to error codes: ERR_SUBPROCESS (103), ERR_PROVIDER_DOWN (28), ERR_TIMEOUT (13), ERR_PROVIDER (17), ERR_AUTH (3), ERR_NESTED_SESSION, ERR_RATE_LIMIT, etc. Each has recovery guidance attached.

worked_well.item: JSONL-based queryable history with aggregation
worked_well.evidence: history.py provides load_history(), query_history(model/status/round_name/task_name), and aggregate_by_model() for cost tracking. Audit records support: dispatch_id, task_name, model, status, cost_usd, input_tokens, output_tokens, duration_sec, error_code.

worked_well.item: Atomic write protection against crash-induced torn reads
worked_well.evidence: audit.py atomic_write() uses temp file + os.replace, preventing partial writes. Finding #210 documented. fcntl.flock in _append_jsonl prevents concurrent writes from parallel threads.

worked_well.item: Secret scrubbing before audit storage
worked_well.evidence: dispatch.py calls sanitize_task_result() and _sanitize_module.sanitize_text() before audit_trail.record_outcome() writes. Result files store secrets_scrubbed count (e.g., dsp_0aed316993fe4a83.result.json shows 1 secret scrubbed).

worked_well.item: Smart return JSON auto-rating (optional)
worked_well.evidence: dispatch.py lines 742-750: validate_return_json() called on done status to populate json_valid and fields_complete fields. 420 done records have json_valid populated; records pre-dating smart_return feature have null.

worked_well.item: Per-model cost aggregation and daily JSONL rollover
worked_well.evidence: history.py log_dispatch() writes one file per day (history-YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl). aggregate_by_model() sums cost/count/duration/success/error per model. audit.py auto-rotates JSONL when size exceeds max_jsonl_bytes (10MB default).

worked_well.item: Cross-process and thread-safe append with file locking
worked_well.evidence: audit.py rotate() and _append_jsonl() use fcntl.flock() (Finding #251, #209) to prevent cross-process race conditions. RONDO-204 Finding #231 documents thread lock. Best-effort fallback on Windows.

worked_well.item: Request_id correlation for cross-retry tracing
worked_well.evidence: RONDO-211 Finding #259: audit.py stores request_id in AuditRecord (line 162). 473 done records have request_id populated; propagated from INTENT to OUTCOME (line 326). Enables tracing retries across invocations.

worked_well.item: Stuck INTENT reconciliation on process startup
worked_well.evidence: audit.py reconcile_stuck_intents() (lines 558-614) detects dispatch_ids with INTENT but no OUTCOME. Respects stuck_after_sec threshold (default 300s) to avoid false-positives on peer workers. 3 stuck records in dataset prove it's working.

friction.item: Cost data missing for all error and subprocess timeout cases
friction.evidence: Queried rondo_audit.jsonl: error_with_cost=0, done_with_cost=475/580, blocked_with_cost=44/44. Error records show cost_usd=0.0, input_tokens=0, output_tokens=0 even when duration_sec > 0. dispatch.py doesn't capture usage on failure path — cost populated only after API call completes successfully.

friction.item: json_valid and fields_complete fields added late, sparse coverage
friction.evidence: 164 error records have json_valid=null (all), done records split: 420/580 have json_valid != null (74%), 160/580 have null. smart_return validate_return_json() only runs if status=='done' (dispatch.py line 742), skipping error/partial/blocked. Coverage started mid-April; March records are all null.

friction.item: Request_id missing in early audit records, retroactively sparse
friction.evidence: 112/164 error records missing request_id (empty string). Only 473/580 done records have populated request_id. RONDO-214 C-3 fix (dispatch.py lines 322-326) added request_id propagation AFTER many records written. No backfill for old records.

friction.item: Prompt and result files stored separately; JSONL doesn't embed them
friction.evidence: audit.py stores prompt_file (e.g., dsp_XXX.prompt.txt) and result_file (e.g., dsp_XXX.result.json) as filenames in JSONL records, not inline. prompt_file empty in OUTCOME records. Consumers must do second I/O to read prompt/result. Scales poorly for large result sets.

friction.item: Error records missing any diagnostic context beyond error_code
friction.evidence: Error records have exit_code (0 or 1) and error_code (e.g., ERR_SUBPROCESS) but no stderr snippet, CLI invocation details, or environment context. Result files exist but are only populated if code ran far enough to produce output. 103 ERR_SUBPROCESS records provide no hint what went wrong.

failures.item: Subprocess errors (ERR_SUBPROCESS) dominate failure cohort but are opaque
failures.evidence: 103/164 errors are ERR_SUBPROCESS (exit code 1). Error records show cost_usd=0, duration_sec=0.3-0.4s (too fast to be real work — likely early crash in subprocess init). No stderr captured in audit trail or result files. classify_error() in dispatch_parse.py line 158 maps exit_code==1 to ERR_SUBPROCESS broadly.
failures.root_cause_guess: dispatch.py subprocess launch probably exits on: missing claude binary (Rondo-REQ-100 req 071), permission issue, missing file, or bad args. stderr from subprocess.run() not saved to audit. Could add stderr capture to error records.

failures.item: Provider errors (ERR_PROVIDER_DOWN=28, ERR_PROVIDER=17) show 0 tokens and $0 cost
failures.evidence: All 45 provider-related errors have cost_usd=0.0, input_tokens=0, output_tokens=0, duration_sec varies (0.3s to 30s). provider-side failures detected after subprocess completes but before API response parsed. Result files missing for some.
failures.root_cause_guess: dispatch.py calls DispatchUsage() with default 0 tokens when parsing fails (line 629-630). Provider returns error response; rondo classifies it but doesn't extract token counts from error payload. Missing: parse API error responses to extract actual token usage.

failures.item: Timeout errors (13 records, some >150s) not distinguished from blocked status
failures.evidence: ERR_TIMEOUT records show duration_sec=152s-164s, cost_usd=0, tokens=0, but status='error' not 'blocked'. Blocked records (44 total) have cost_usd>0 and tokens>0, implying API succeeded. Timeout detection happens after process.wait(timeout=X), not reflected in cost/tokens fields.
failures.root_cause_guess: Timeout fires in subprocess or watchdog, subprocess already exited. dispatch.py line 591 sets error_code='ERR_TIMEOUT' but doesn't retroactively fill cost/tokens — they're only populated on successful API response (line 351).

failures.item: Stuck INTENT records show 0s duration and no completion metadata
failures.evidence: 3 stuck records: dispatched_at filled, completed_at added by reconcile (not during dispatch), duration_sec=0.0, cost=0, tokens=0, files_modified=[]. reconcile_stuck_intents() synthesizes OUTCOME with status='stuck' and ERR_RECONCILED_STUCK but can't populate true duration or cost.
failures.root_cause_guess: Stuck records indicate process crash before OUTCOME recorded. True duration is now - dispatched_at (minutes in some cases), but audit records it as duration=0 since no timing was captured. Reconciliation fills timestamp gap but not duration.

feature_gaps.item: No capture of provider HTTP status codes or API error details
feature_gaps.why: dispatch.py parses provider response but doesn't log HTTP status, error message, or error type (429 vs 500 vs malformed response) to audit. Blocked status records have cost but not detailed error reason. Makes root-cause triage hard for provider failures.

feature_gaps.item: No per-step timing breakdown (subprocess startup vs API latency vs response parse)
feature_gaps.why: dispatch.py measures wall-clock duration_sec only. No breakdown: how long for subprocess startup? API call? result parsing? Timeout detection is coarse (either fires or doesn't). Can't isolate where 10s delays come from.

feature_gaps.item: Prompt_file empty in OUTCOME records; prompt not stored inline in audit for analysis
feature_gaps.why: audit.py records prompt_file in INTENT (line 251), blank in OUTCOME (line 179 to_dict() sets empty). If audit.jsonl is the 'source of truth', prompt is orphaned in filesystem. No way to correlate prompt hash to full text retroactively from JSONL alone.

feature_gaps.item: No model capability hints or retry-ability markers in error records
feature_gaps.why: classify_error() returns error_code and recovery guidance, but audit records don't store is_retryable or is_transient flags. Consumers must hardcode retry logic per error_code. dispatch.py get_error_recovery() (line 423) has this info but it's not persisted.

feature_gaps.item: No tenant isolation validation in audit records; cross-tenant leakage possible
feature_gaps.why: RONDO-200 Finding #217 noted tenant_id should be in audit for isolation. audit.py _get_tenant_for_audit() computes it (line 53) but doesn't store it in AuditRecord. Tenant is only in directory path (~/.rondo/audit/{tenant}). If directory is breached, no audit_id field proves which tenant a record belongs to.

feature_gaps.item: No distributed tracing fields (parent_request_id, trace_id, span_id) for multi-hop scenarios
feature_gaps.why: request_id added for cross-retry correlation, but no trace_id for multi-dispatch workflows. If task A dispatches task B, no way to trace lineage in audit. RONDO-211 C-3 covers request_id only.

feature_gaps.item: No dispatch arguments (model, do/read/done, --auth, --timeout) recorded in audit
feature_gaps.why: audit_record has task_name, model, but not the full CLI args or config that produced the dispatch. If model changes between runs, can't compare cost/duration fairly. task.py Task dataclass and config not stored.

feature_gaps.item: Result files contain raw_output + secrets_scrubbed count but not sanitization metadata (which patterns matched, confidence scores)
feature_gaps.why: dispatch.py calls sanitize_text() but only stores count in result_file (line 354). No log of which secrets were found, confidence, or pattern names. Can't audit if scrubbing is over/under-aggressive.

p2.area: Claude Code (CLI) — April–June 2026 Features & RONDO Relevance

features.name: Dynamic Workflows (Multi-Agent Orchestration)
features.summary: Claude writes JavaScript orchestration scripts dynamically to coordinate 16 concurrent agents (max 1,000 total). Subagents validate each other's claims adversarially. Proven: rewrote Bun (750k lines) Zig→Rust in 11 days with 99.8% test pass rate.
features.suggested_use: RONDO should offer `--backend=workflows` flag to translate dispatch tasks into Dynamic Workflow definitions and execute via Claude API instead of raw provider calls. This handles RONDO's `rondo_cloud(..., count=N)` / `rondo_multi_review` patterns natively in Claude Code.

features.name: Agent Teams & Subagents (Worker Coordination)
features.summary: Agent teams: multiple Claude Code sessions coordinate via shared task lists; agents message each other. Subagents: YAML-defined workers (custom prompt + tools) run within parent session, token-efficient. Launched Feb 2026 with Opus 4.6 (experimental).
features.suggested_use: RONDO should emit subagent YAML definitions and orchestrate via Claude Code agent teams for long-running parallel work. Keeps RONDO in orchestration layer while delegating session/context management to Claude Code.

features.name: Structured Output with JSON Schema
features.summary: Define JSON Schema; agent returns validated output matching that schema. Supports Zod (TypeScript) and Pydantic (Python). No post-processing needed; schema enforced end-to-end.
features.suggested_use: Make `output_format` a first-class RONDO parameter. Require schemas for machine-readable results to eliminate custom parsing and ensure result reliability across providers.

features.name: Prompt Caching (5-Minute TTL, Workspace Isolation)
features.summary: 84% cache hit rate documented (74% cost savings). Input tokens served from cache reduce $100 session cost to $19 at 90% hit rate. TTL shortened 60 min → 5 min (early 2026); caches now isolated per workspace.
features.suggested_use: Auto-enable prompt caching for multi-provider reviews where same context is sent to Gemini, Grok, Anthropic. Cache aggressively for interactive dispatch; skip for overnight batches (5-min TTL won't survive). Track cache efficiency per provider.

features.name: 1 Million Token Context Window
features.summary: Opus 4.6 & Sonnet 4.6 support 1M context (≈830K usable tokens = full monorepo + docs). No premium cost beyond 200K. Auto-enabled on Max/Team/Enterprise for Opus. Maintains entity relationships; 78.3% MRCR v2 accuracy.
features.suggested_use: Expose 1M context as config option. Map RONDO effort levels to context size: low=200K, med=500K, high=1M. Enable full-codebase analysis in single agent invocation without context thrashing.

features.name: MCP Server Improvements & Background Task Fixes
features.summary: Fixed `/mcp tools list` rendering for long tool names. `/bg while responding` continues in background without dropping. Fixed temp file permission prompts. Dynamic Workflows integrate seamlessly with MCP.
features.suggested_use: Rely on Claude Code's native MCP server diagnostics instead of duplicating validation. If RONDO implements its own MCP server, wire it into Claude Code workflows for dispatch feedback and result streaming.

features.name: Hooks Automation (21 Lifecycle Events)
features.summary: 21 lifecycle hooks (pre/post tool execution, session start/end, etc.). Command hooks receive JSON on stdin; structured JSON output on stdout. Enable auto-run shell commands, integrations, safety checks on every tool invocation.
features.suggested_use: Document how RONDO workflows integrate with Claude Code hooks. Example: submit rondo_run task in hook, consume overnight results via rondo_spool_consume on session_end hook. Automates dispatch without RONDO managing session lifecycle.

p2.area: Anthropic API Opus 4.8 Features for RONDO Integration

features.name: Adaptive Thinking (type: 'adaptive')
features.summary: Claude Opus 4.8 only supports adaptive thinking mode — manual thinking with budget_tokens is rejected with HTTP 400. Adaptive thinking auto-determines when/how much to use extended thinking per-request; no budget_tokens parameter allowed.
features.suggested_use: RONDO adapters for Opus 4.8 must use thinking: {type: 'adaptive'} (or omit thinking entirely if disabled). Remove any code sending thinking: {type: 'enabled', budget_tokens: N}. Interleaved thinking automatically enabled on Opus 4.8.

features.name: Output Config + Effort Parameter (output_config.effort)
features.summary: The effort parameter (low/medium/high/xhigh/max) controls thinking depth & token spend across text, tool calls, and reasoning. Replaces manual budget_tokens. Available on Opus 4.8, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.5. At high/xhigh/max, Claude almost always thinks; at low/medium, may skip for simple tasks.
features.suggested_use: RONDO adapters should accept effort in output_config: {effort: 'medium'|'high'|'xhigh'|'max'}. Combine with adaptive thinking for best control: thinking: {type: 'adaptive'} + output_config: {effort: 'xhigh'}. Effort=high is default (omitting = same behavior). For coding/agentic, start with xhigh.

features.name: Temperature/top_p/top_k Rejection (HTTP 400 Fix)
features.summary: Opus 4.8 rejects temperature, top_p, top_k parameters entirely—any of these in request body returns 400 error. Adaptive thinking has replaced sampling control; these fields are incompatible.
features.suggested_use: RONDO HTTP adapters must STRIP temperature, top_p, top_k entirely from request payloads before sending to Opus 4.8/4.7. Don't conditionally send—omit them. Check adapters for any param-building logic that adds these fields.

features.name: Anthropic-Version Header
features.summary: All Anthropic API requests require anthropic-version header (e.g., 2023-06-01). SDK handles this automatically; HTTP requests must include it explicitly. Current standard version is 2023-06-01.
features.suggested_use: RONDO HTTP adapter must set header: 'anthropic-version: 2023-06-01' on all requests. SDK clients (python anthropic, JS @anthropic-ai/sdk) handle this automatically.

features.name: 1M Token Context Window
features.summary: Opus 4.8, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6 all support 1M token context at standard pricing (no premium). 200k on Microsoft Foundry. No additional cost for full context utilization.
features.suggested_use: RONDO can safely submit requests up to 1M tokens of context without cost penalty. Useful for large codebase analysis, batch document processing, in-context learning.

features.name: Prompt Caching (5-min & 1-hour durations)
features.summary: Cache hits cost 10% of input token price. Write costs ~1.25x after 5 min, 2x after 1 hr. Opus 4.8 supports prompt caching. Batch API supports caching with best-effort 30-98% hit rates depending on traffic patterns.
features.suggested_use: RONDO can include cache_control: {type: 'ephemeral'} blocks in system/context for shared content across multiple requests. For batches, use 1-hour cache duration for better hit rates (since batches process asynchronously).

features.name: Message Batches API (50% Cost Reduction)
features.summary: Async batch processing: submit up to 100k requests per batch or 256MB max. Most complete <1hr. All params supported except stream, speed (fast mode), store, cache_hint, context_hint, max_tokens:0. 50% discount on input+output tokens.
features.suggested_use: RONDO should add batch() mode for overnight/non-urgent tasks. Support thinking, effort, caching in batches. Each request must have custom_id (1-64 chars alphanumeric/hyphen/underscore). Pricing: Opus 4.8 batch = $2.50 in / $12.50 out per MTok (vs $5/$25 regular).

features.name: Dynamic Workflows + Claude Code Integration
features.summary: Claude Code now supports dynamic workflows (multi-agent, codebase-scale migrations). Opus 4.8 powers large-scale transformations. Claude Agent SDK separates billing from interactive Claude (June 15 2026: Pro=$20/mo credit, Max5x=$100, Max20x=$200 Agent credit, separate pool).
features.suggested_use: RONDO can delegate complex multi-step tasks to Claude Code subprocess mode (--auth max) or Agent SDK. Separate billing pool for agent work; monitor Jun 15 billing split. Use rondo_cloud with profile=coding for leverage.

features.name: Thinking Display Control (display: 'summarized' vs 'omitted')
features.summary: On Opus 4.8/4.7, thinking.display defaults to 'omitted' (empty thinking field, signature still carries encrypted full thinking for multi-turn). Set display: 'summarized' to see thinking text. Omitted mode = faster time-to-first-token when streaming.
features.suggested_use: For RONDO, typically omit thinking from responses (use display: 'omitted'). Only set display: 'summarized' if user explicitly wants to see reasoning. Both modes charged full thinking tokens—display choice affects latency, not cost.

features.name: Fast Mode (2.5x speed, 3x cheaper than Opus 4.7)
features.summary: Fast mode parameter (speed: 'low'/'default'/'high') NOT supported in batch requests. Available for sync Messages API only. Opus 4.8 fast: $10 in / $50 out per MTok (was much pricier on 4.7).
features.suggested_use: RONDO interactive (non-batch) requests can opt into fast mode for speed-sensitive tasks, but batches cannot use it. Mentioned for completeness; not a blocker for current dispatch flows.

features.name: System Messages in Messages Array (Mid-Conversation)
features.summary: Messages API now accepts system entries inside the messages array (not just top-level), allowing dynamic instruction updates mid-conversation. Feature for advanced agent workflows.
features.suggested_use: RONDO multi-turn agentic flows can inject system prompt updates between assistant/user turns. Useful for effort escalation or context refinement mid-dispatch. Optional feature; standard top-level system still works.

features.name: Structured Outputs (format parameter in output_config)
features.summary: output_config can include format: {type: 'json', schema: {...}} for JSON schema validation. Combines with effort parameter in same output_config object. Opus 4.8 stable API (no beta header needed).
features.suggested_use: RONDO adapters can request structured JSON responses via output_config: {format: {type: 'json', schema: {...}}, effort: 'high'}. Useful for parsing dispatch results, ensuring schema compliance.

p3.what_it_does: The Claude Agent SDK is a Python/TypeScript library that packages Claude Code (the interactive development environment) as a programmable framework for building multi-turn autonomous AI agents. It provides spawned subprocess execution of the Claude Code CLI, built-in tool execution via MCP protocol, persistent session state management with automatic disk persistence and resumption, and provider routing through ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (with fallback to AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, and Anthropic Foundry). The SDK emphasizes agent autonomy with Claude handling tool calls and control flow internally; it is designed primarily for interactive, long-running agent workflows with strong codebase interaction (file editing, command execution, reasoning).
strengths: Deep MCP integration with in-process custom tool support via @tool decorators
Multi-turn persistent session state with automatic resumption and fork/continue/resume lifecycle hooks
Native code editing, command execution, and codebase exploration built into Claude Code
First-party support from Anthropic with tight Claude Code UI/CLI/SDK ecosystem
Lifecycle hooks (on_tool_use, on_message, etc.) for fine-grained agent loop control
Supports streaming input and single-response modes for different use cases
Cloud provider routing (Bedrock, Vertex AI, Foundry) in addition to Anthropic API
weaknesses: Requires Node.js 18+ (CLI binary bundled but external runtime dependency)
Tightly coupled to Claude Code — limited flexibility for alternative providers
Spawns subprocess (same as RONDO) but with less control over retry/circuit-breaker/cost-gating semantics
Persistent session state is powerful for interactive workflows but adds complexity for stateless batch/overnight automation
No multi-provider consensus, cost-aware routing, or provider-agnostic abstraction layer
Authentication model routes through Anthropic API key or cloud provider auth — no Max plan $0 option documented
Designed for interactive agent development, not for scripted dispatch workflows with structured output contracts
No built-in audit trail, metrics, retry history, or dispatch result comparison features
p3.overlap_with_rondo: Both RONDO and Claude Agent SDK spawn the Claude Code CLI as a subprocess and communicate via structured messages. Both provide MCP integration for tool access. Both support multi-turn workflows and can route through Anthropic API. Both abstract away direct prompt engineering to some degree. The sdkNote: Simon Willison's `llm` tool overlaps in that it provides a multi-provider CLI for AI dispatch and supports tool calling via plugins, but it is fundamentally a command-line repl with logging/embedding persistence, not a programmatic dispatch framework — it lacks RONDO's structured task contracts, circuit breakers, retry logic, cost gating, overnight automation, and multi-provider consensus routing. The `llm` tool is mentioned here as it is a notable CLI alternative in the space but is not the primary comparison subject.
p3.rondo_advantage: RONDO's durable competitive advantages over the Claude Agent SDK are: (1) **Provider-agnostic abstraction**: routes to Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Grok, Mistral, Ollama via unified adapter layer without rewriting dispatch code; (2) **$0 Max plan authentication**: can dispatch to Claude Code with zero API cost by leveraging Claude Code's Max plan subscription (Claude Agent SDK requires API key auth); (3) **Minimal dependencies**: only requires mcp>=1.0 and PyYAML>=6.0, no external runtime (vs. Node.js requirement); (4) **Overnight/batch automation**: built-in phase scheduling, event logging, cost monitoring, and spool-based background dispatch that Agent SDK leaves to the host; (5) **Structured dispatch contracts**: Three-field Do/Read/Done model with output validation, error classification, and result schemas that make workflows repeatable and auditable; (6) **Cost-aware execution**: circuit breakers, budget caps, health metrics, and retry logic with configurable thresholds; (7) **Audit and observability**: dispatch history, metrics aggregation, diff-based regression checking, and cost-per-model reporting; (8) **Execution mode separation**: explicit control over inline (host planning), subprocess (independent execution), or agent (plan return for host spawn) vs. Agent SDK's implicit subprocess model.
gaps_rondo_should_close: Add first-class session resumption hooks (Agent SDK's continue/fork/resume pattern) to make long-running workflows with state carry-over more ergonomic than current 'context-passing' approach

Implement native Claude Code interactive mode integration so RONDO tasks can invoke Claude Code as a subprocess in interactive-repl mode (vs. current pipe-json model), useful for exploratory agent workflows
Document and optimize for TypeScript/Node.js parity; currently Python-first with limited TypeScript support, while Agent SDK targets both equally
Add streaming response support for long-form outputs (current model collects full result before returning), useful for real-time feedback in interactive MCP scenarios
Build tighter integration with Claude Code projects/ session store so RONDO tasks can resume broken workflows without explicit state management
Publish benchmark suite comparing provider latency, cost, and accuracy across identical dispatch rounds to compete with Agent SDK's credibility in enterprise adoption
Expand custom tool ecosystem beyond current MCP; consider a lighter-weight decorator pattern like Agent SDK's @tool to lower barrier for inline tool definition
Add native support for Agent SDK's new lifecycle hooks (on_tool_use, on_message, on_response) to allow fine-grained observability hooks in RONDO dispatch loops

p3.what_it_does: Aider is an open-source terminal-based AI pair-programming tool that helps developers code by reading a Git repository, sending relevant file context to an LLM (Claude, GPT-4, Gemini, DeepSeek, or local models), applying edits, and auto-committing changes with descriptive messages. It operates interactively in the terminal, making codebase maps for context efficiency, and supports multiple languages, git integration, voice input, image/webpage context injection, and lint-and-test fixing.
strengths: Git-native workflow with atomic, reviewable commits for each AI change
Multi-provider LLM support (Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek, Ollama) via LiteLLM
Comprehensive repository context mapping (Tree-sitter based) for intelligent multi-file edits
Voice-to-code, image/webpage context injection, and visual reference support
Full open-source (free software, you only pay API providers)
Interactive terminal UX optimized for developer workflow
Lint-and-test fixing loops to validate changes
Prompt caching support (Claude/OpenAI) for cost savings
Unlimited session history in markdown transcripts for review and sharing
Mid-chat model switching capability
weaknesses: Not designed for headless automation or batch processing—requires interactive developer guidance between steps
No built-in test-fix iteration loops or feedback loops beyond linting
Limited to interactive single-session workflows—no parallel agent support or orchestration
No circuit breaker, retry logic, or resilience patterns for API failures
Requires developer to stay present to guide direction—cannot run overnight autonomously
No cost capping, budget tracking, or multi-tier execution (high/default/low effort) options
Weak audit trail—only markdown chat history and optional llm-history file (no structured dispatch history or metrics dashboard)
No scheduling, cron automation, or task queue for recurring work
Cannot handle complex multi-step workflows where each step branches on results
Analytics only via PostHog (external, not built-in structured logging)
p3.overlap_with_rondo: Both are CLI-first tools for AI dispatch without requiring an IDE or GUI. Both support multiple LLM providers and focus on developer-friendly workflows. Both log interaction history (Aider via markdown transcripts, RONDO via structured audit/history). Both emphasize non-interactive use in some form (Aider has headless mode for CI/CD, RONDO is designed for it).
p3.rondo_advantage: RONDO is purpose-built for autonomous overnight batch dispatch and orchestration, while Aider requires interactive direction. RONDO offers circuit breaker/retry logic, cost capping, multi-tier effort levels (low/med/high/max), dual auth modes (subscription $0 vs pay-per-token), structured dispatch history/audit trails with metrics, job scheduling/cron support, and the ability to chain complex multi-step workflows where each step feeds output to the next. RONDO supports parallel dispatches to multiple providers for comparison and can run unattended for hours/days, whereas Aider is optimized for a human sitting at the terminal guiding edits in a single session. RONDO's three-field Do/Read/Done contract makes it composable for orchestration across projects (OB, Caliber, ACE, USH research).
gaps_rondo_should_close: Add interactive terminal UI mode (Aider-style scrollback + chat) for developers who want to stay in the loop—currently RONDO is dispatch-heavy, not conversational
Implement native Git integration layer (auto-commit, branch creation, diff review) like Aider's for code-focused workflows—currently RONDO is task-agnostic
Build in lint-and-test loop automation (detect failures, re-prompt, iterate) for code tasks—Aider has this, RONDO does not
Add codebase mapping/context intelligence (Tree-sitter or similar) to reduce token waste and improve relevance on large repos—Aider does this, RONDO relies on user-specified context
Support voice input and multimodal context injection (images/webpages)—Aider has these, RONDO is text-only dispatch
Create a web UI for reviewing dispatch results in real-time (complementing CLI-first design)—useful for overnight jobs that finish while asleep

p3.what_it_does: RONDO is a zero-external-dependency Python framework for dispatching AI tasks to multiple providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Ollama, Mistral, Groq) with structured output contracts. Declarative task definitions (YAML), repeatable execution modes (inline/subprocess/agent), parallel + overnight automation, cost control, audit trails, and MCP server interface for Claude Code integration. Designed for operational workflows (code review, fixes, batch processing) where reliability, cost visibility, and 24/7 unattended automation matter more than conversation-driven reasoning.
strengths: Zero external dependencies (stdlib + 2 packages: mcp, PyYAML) — lightweight, minimal supply chain risk, fast install
Explicit cost control: effort levels (low/med/high/max), budget caps, rate limit gating, overnight overage handling, daily cost tracking
Overnight automation: phase orchestration, self-healing watchdog (kills hung tasks on output silence), morning reports, spool mailbox pattern for disconnected consumers
Multiple execution modes decouple caller from runner: inline (planning), subprocess (isolated), agent (Claude AI spawning), provider API (HTTP adapter)
Structured output contracts enforce consistent data boundaries; error envelope (Rondo-STD-108) standardizes failure modes
MCP server integration with Claude Code (Rondo-IFS-104): dispatch via tool calls; 14 tools for dispatch/status/history/retry/diff/analyze
Parallel dispatch with conflict detection + optional worktree isolation per task
Subprocess safety: CLAUDECODE env stripped, --bare flag (headless), no interactive input required
Result spool (mailbox pattern) for unattended batch pickup; TTL cleanup; stateless persistence (no database)
Transparent audit trail: dispatch history, cost per model, reliability metrics (success rate, token counts)
Result formats: structured JSON by default; dry-run preview before execution
Three-field contract: Do (what to run) / Read (what data needed) / Done (when done)
weaknesses: Limited to prompt-in/structured-output-out; no stateful multi-turn conversation loops (by design — conversation is interactive)
No built-in tool ecosystem (hundreds of tools). Tools are caller's responsibility (Rondo routes prompts, doesn't execute tools)
No RAG/vector database integration (orthogonal concern; Rondo doesn't manage knowledge bases)
No native human-in-the-loop UI (design decision: overnight runs must be unattended; human checkpoints handled by caller)
Graph-based orchestration limited (Rondo is task dispatch, not state machine graphs); for complex branching logic, caller must orchestrate via code
Streaming output is provided for interactive dispatch but not optimized for token-by-token UI updates (result batched from subprocess)
No memory persistence across sessions (stateless by design; caller manages session state if needed)
Learning curve for YAML round file syntax + execution mode semantics; not as intuitive as role-based frameworks
Small ecosystem compared to LangChain + LangGraph (no third-party integrations yet)
Overnight scheduling is local-only (cron/LaunchAgent); no cloud scheduler support
No native container/distributed execution (dispatches via Claude CLI subprocess; scales horizontally via parallel workers, not containers)
p3.overlap_with_rondo: RONDO directly competes with LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen in orchestrating multi-model AI tasks. All four frameworks enable: (1) routing work to multiple AI providers, (2) structured result contracts, (3) error handling + retries, (4) cost tracking. All support Python async/parallel execution. LangGraph and RONDO both emphasize deterministic, auditable execution; CrewAI emphasizes developer velocity; AutoGen emphasizes dialogue-driven reasoning. RONDO's differentiator is zero external dependencies + overnight unattended automation + cost gating (critical for Mark's 24/7 research cycles on Usher Syndrome and ACE automation).
p3.rondo_advantage: RONDO's niche advantage: (1) **Zero-dependency dispatch** — install Rondo, Python 3.12+, and you dispatch immediately. LangGraph/CrewAI/AutoGen all require heavy dependency stacks (langchain, pydantic, requests, etc.). (2) **Overnight automation with cost control** — RONDO's only framework designed for unattended 6-hour batch runs with usage gating, watchdog, rate limit backoff. LangGraph is interactive/observable; CrewAI and AutoGen are conversation-driven. (3) **MCP-first design** — RONDO's Rondo-IFS-104 spec embeds 14 MCP tools natively into Claude Code workflow. LangGraph's LangSmith is separate platform/cost. (4) **Operational simplicity** — YAML task definitions, no graph config syntax; execution modes hide where/how code runs from the caller. (5) **Subprocess safety** — CLAUDECODE stripping, --bare headless mode, explicit auth switching (Max plan vs API key) prevent nested session hangs and API key leaks. (6) **Cost transparency** — RONDO tracks cost per model, effort level, daily spend, overage events; baked into every dispatch (Rondo-STD-105). LangGraph/CrewAI require external monitoring.
gaps_rondo_should_close: Built-in tool ecosystem: RONDO dispatches text; it doesn't execute tools. Borrowing CrewAI's approach (E2B sandbox, MCP tool server, code execution wrappers) would unlock 'dispatch agent code + get back results' without caller writing tool wrapper loops. ~80-120 hours to integrate 5 canonical tools (web search, code execution, file ops, vector DB query, shell).

Graph-based branching: RONDO-REQ-101 adds parallel + overnight, but no conditional edges. If task A fails, run B else run C. Currently caller must orchestrate via code/Python API. LangGraph's conditional_edges pattern is powerful. Adding opt-in DAG branching (Rondo-REQ-201?) would let YAML rounds declare: task_a -> [success: task_b, failure: task_c]. ~40-60 hours.

Stream-optimized output: Subprocess dispatch batches results; interactive dispatch doesn't stream tokens live. For interactive REPL review loops (Claude Code), token-by-token streaming to UI is expected. Rondo-REQ-107 (streaming spec) planned but not implemented. ~30-40 hours.

Session memory / multi-turn context stacking: RONDO is single-shot dispatch. For iterative workflows ('review code, AI fixes, review again'), caller manages turn-by-turn context. Adding a Context Stack layer (Rondo-REQ-108) with implicit multi-turn message threading would enable 'ask same agent to iterate' without explicit round-per-turn. ~60-80 hours.

Container / distributed scaling: RONDO scales to ~4 parallel workers via ThreadPoolExecutor. For 100+ concurrent dispatches, caller must orchestrate multiple Rondo instances. K8s/container-ready patterns (task queue, worker pods) would enable large-scale overnight runs. ~120-180 hours.

Hosted platform / cloud scheduler: RONDO's overnight runs are local (cron/LaunchAgent). Offering 'schedule round file on Rondo cloud' (like LangSmith) would unlock non-technical user access. ~160-200 hours + hosting costs.

RAG / knowledge base context injection: RONDO doesn't query vector stores or manage knowledge bases. Adding optional Retrieval Gate (Rondo-REQ-109?) that fetches context before dispatch ('pass top-3 docs from Pinecone into prompt') would support document-grounded tasks. ~40-60 hours.

Human-in-the-loop UI: RONDO's checkpoint is callback-based (caller's responsibility). Adding a lightweight approval web UI ('click to resume after AI output') would unlock interactive overnight workflows. ~80-120 hours.

synth.executive_summary: RONDO has achieved strong foundational stability and operational maturity in 8 weeks (102 commits, 1747 passing tests, 68.5% dispatch success rate). Core strengths: zero-dependency dispatch, cost-aware multi-provider routing, overnight automation, audit trail completeness, and MCP integration. Competitive position is secure against LangGraph/CrewAI in the "unattended batch AI" niche. However, three critical blockers prevent production-scale reliability: (1) Opus 4.8 adapter incompatibility (temperature field + missing effort parameter), (2) Subprocess authentication fragility (13% malformed JSON, "Not logged in" failures), (3) Distributed auto_reconcile race condition (INTENT/IN-FLIGHT/DONE state machine not atomic). Additionally, 5 feature gaps (provider registry auto-refresh, effort-level API exposure, distributed idempotency, structured logging adoption, HTTP retry/breaker parity) are preventing USH research and downstream adoption. Recommendations below prioritize: P0 (stability/bugs), P1 (high-value, unblocks research), P2 (nice-to-have ecosystem polish). Effort estimates account for existing test infrastructure and partial implementations (RONDO-209-295 work provides foundation).
synth.rondo_niche: RONDO owns the "structured, cost-aware, unattended multi-provider AI dispatch framework" niche. Competitors (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen) target interactive agent reasoning; Claude Agent SDK targets interactive MCP workflows; Aider targets developer pair-programming. RONDO's monopoly: (1) zero external dependencies (fastest to install/security), (2) $0 Max plan auth mode (cost control for night-shift labs), (3) built-in overnight phase scheduling + watchdog + spool mailbox (no cron/job-queue learning curve), (4) cost gating + circuit breakers (protect budget-constrained research), (5) three-field Do/Read/Done contract + audit trail (repeatable workflows across projects). Defensibility: low barriers to copy individual features, but ecosystem lock-in is weak.
